spec_match.rationale: Demonstrates the exact pattern the spec calls out — Atomics.pause(1 << Math.min(attempt, 6)) — and explains the linear/exponential-backoff framing from the spec text.

interactive.rationale: Configurable wait duration + run button; two columns show iterations-per-window and a small bar chart of per-bucket iteration counts.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Blurb describes a tight-vs-backoff iteration comparison across a fixed window — that's what the page shows.

edge_cases.rationale: Doesn't cap on the spec's clarification that implementations clamp N to a sensible internal range (tens-to-hundreds of nanoseconds) — the comment in the code mentions it but the demo never tries a pathological N (e.g. 1 << 20) to prove the clamp.

design_system.rationale: Variables throughout, no raw hex, chart bars use --text-black.

openQuestions.title: Show the implementation clamp
openQuestions.detail: The page asserts the engine clamps the N argument 'to a sensible internal range, so go wild', but never proves it. Add a third column that uses pause(1 << 20) per iteration — it should take the same wall-clock time as pause(1 << 6), showing the clamp is real.

openQuestions.title: Plot iterations over time, not just buckets
openQuestions.detail: The bar chart is iterations sampled across the wait window — labelling the X axis (time bins) and showing the two series overlaid on the same chart would make the 'tight burns CPU; backoff coasts' story instantly readable.

summary: Resolved the required ChromeStatus reference; 2 follow-up question(s) remain.
